text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 19 January 2019 

Dr Martin Hale  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Martin Hale,                                                                                                         

Daniel Cooper, DOB: 22-11-1991, Hospital No: H673920 NHS No. 9182736450  
Flat 4, 27 Meadow Lane, London, SE1 9PX  

Epilepsy 

Medication: Ongoing lamotrigine therapy commenced November 2018 following prior lapse in adherence. 

Lamotrigine 50 mg in the morning and 100 mg at night (patient-reported, using standard tablets). 

Lamotrigine Blood Level: 

14th January 2019: 2.8 mg/L (reference range 1.0–15.0 mg/L). Dose at time of sample: 50 mg AM, 100 mg PM.  

Seizures: His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with six tonic seizures documented in 2019 so far. However, both the patient and his mother report that occupational demands are contributing to worsening control day-to-day. He works in a care setting with irregular breaks and frequent patient transfers; cumulative fatigue has been noted with perceived worsening, particularly on late shifts and after clusters of physically demanding days. No consistent aura or warning has been identified. Reported post-ictal confusion lasting up to 20 minutes, with occasional myalgia following events. 

Today’s review was conducted by telephone with the patient and his mother present on speaker. They describe more frequent near-miss episodes at work (brief blank spells and startle-type jerks) over the past month, which they attribute to sleep disruption and missed rest periods. There have been two work-related incidents in which he required assistance to sit safely due to sudden loss of tone; neither involved head injury, but both have increased his anxiety. Despite the noted improvement compared with previous years in absolute numbers, the current pattern suggests deterioration in day-to-day stability under occupational strain. He wishes to continue working but recognises that the current rota and manual handling intensity may be exacerbating symptoms. 

He reports good tolerance of lamotrigine with no rash, dizziness, or diplopia. Morning doses are occasionally delayed when shifts overrun. He is using a smartphone alarm to support adherence; his mother is keeping a seizure diary and has begun logging sleep duration and shift patterns to correlate events. 

Plan discussed today: We will obtain an updated occupational risk assessment from his employer and request temporary adjustments to allow regular breaks and reduced manual handling where feasible. We have arranged repeat bloods in four weeks, including lamotrigine level, FBC, and U&Es, to monitor for variability with shift patterns. Safety advice reinforced, including avoiding lone patient transfers and ensuring colleagues are informed of immediate first-aid measures. We will review the seizure diary at the next appointment and consider further therapy optimisation if instability persists under adjusted work conditions. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: six tonic seizures documented in 2019 so far
His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with six tonic seizures documented in 2019 so far

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 19 January 2019. The letter states "six tonic seizures documented in 2019 so far," which refers to the period from 1 January 2019 to 19 January 2019 (approximately one month). No other explicit, higher-frequency seizure count is provided. Normalizing this to a monthly rate gives 6 per month.
seizure_frequency_number: six tonic seizures documented in 2019 so far
reference: His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with six tonic seizures documented in 2019 so far
